meta.title: Dendritic Diffusion: Growing Sentences Like Crystals
meta.description: How we turned an autoregressive language model's masked diffusion cousin into a depth-first crystal grower — and why text generation should work more like dendrites than assembly lines
meta.author: Peter Balogh

props.title: 1. The Problem: Why Autoregressive Generation Is a Trap

children.children: GPT-style language models generate text one token at a time, left to right. This is like writing a novel by committing to each word before you know how the sentence — let alone the paragraph — ends.

The garden-path problem: 'The horse raced past the barn fell.' An AR model commits to 'raced' as a main verb. When 'fell' arrives, it's too late to backtrack. The representation was wrong, and there's no mechanism to fix it.
This isn't a minor inconvenience — it's an architectural ceiling. Our earlier work showed the consequences:
Attention heads implement Bloom filters with unavoidable false positives (Anxiety of Influence)
~50% of MLP nonlinearity is spent on tokens that don't need it (Half the Nonlinearity)
MLP routing is binary — consensus or exception — with no gradient in between (Discrete Charm)
L11's 'exception handler' is the model's attempt at backtracking. When it fails, hallucinations propagate downstream (Darkness Visible)

The common thread: the transformer does predictive coding badly because it wasn't designed for it. The exception handler is an emergency hack. What if we could make generation itself a refinement process?

props.title: 2. Diffusion Models: The Other Way to Generate

children.children: You've seen diffusion in image generation (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E). Start with pure noise, iteratively denoise until a clear image appears. The key insight: you don't commit to any pixel until the whole picture comes into focus.

Can we do this with text? Yes — and it already exists. Models like LLaDA and Dream use masked diffusion: start with all tokens masked, then iteratively unmask them based on confidence. The most certain tokens crystallize first.

props.title: How Masked Diffusion Language Models Work

children.children: 1. Start: Every position is a [MASK] token.
2. The model predicts what each mask should be, with confidence scores.
3. Unmask the most confident predictions. Leave uncertain ones masked.
4. Repeat: the model now sees the unmasked tokens as context, refines its predictions for remaining masks.
5. Continue until all positions are unmasked.
This is genuinely different from left-to-right generation. The model can resolve the END of a sentence before the MIDDLE, if the ending is more certain.

Key terminology: We use the word 'crystallize' deliberately. When a mask position resolves to a definite token, it's like a molecule locking into a crystal lattice — the position is frozen, and it constrains what neighboring positions can become.

But existing diffusion language models have their own problem: they generate all positions simultaneously. This is breadth-first — every token denoises independently, and position 5 doesn't 'know' that position 12 just resolved to 'Paris.' For single sentences, this is fine. For paragraphs, it produces garbled, repetitive text.

props.title: 3. The Insight: Crystals Don't Grow Everywhere at Once

children.children: Watch a real crystal grow. It doesn't appear uniformly — it nucleates at a point, then grows outward along preferred directions. A snowflake's first arm implies the hexagonal symmetry of all subsequent arms. The local structure reveals the global plan.
This is the core insight of Dendritic Diffusion. 'Dendritic' means tree-like (Greek δενδρίτης), and it describes both the shape of the generated text and the process that creates it.
Imagine writing an essay about European capitals.

Breadth-first (standard diffusion): Try to write ALL the sentences at once. Every position denoises independently. Result: 'Paris is the capital of France. London is the London of London.' The second sentence garbles because it didn't know the first sentence's structure.

Depth-first (dendritic): Write the first sentence completely: 'Paris is the capital of France.' Now the model SEES this finished sentence as context. Second sentence crystallizes with full awareness: 'London is the capital of the United Kingdom.' Clean, parallel, coherent.
The trick: we get the flexibility of diffusion (no left-to-right commitment) while preserving the coherence of sequential generation (each piece knows what came before).

props.title: 4. How Dendritic Diffusion Actually Works

children.children: The algorithm has three stages that repeat: Crystallize, Measure, Discover.

props.title: Stage 1: Branch Crystallization

children.children: Allocate a block of masked tokens for one 'branch' (roughly one sentence). Run standard masked diffusion, but conditioned on all previously crystallized content.
Prompt: 'Explain how photosynthesis works.'
Step 0: [MASK] [MASK] [MASK] [MASK] [MASK] [MASK] [MASK] [MASK] ...
Step 5: [MASK] [MASK] is [MASK] process [MASK] [MASK] plants [MASK] ...
Step 15: Photosynthesis is the process by which plants convert ...
Notice: 'process' and 'plants' (content words) can resolve before 'the' and 'by which' (function words) — or vice versa, depending on the model's confidence. There is no left-to-right constraint.

props.title: Stage 2: Supersaturation Measurement

children.children: After a branch crystallizes, we ask: 'Is there energy left?' We measure the model's confidence on hypothetical continuation masks — the supersaturation.
High supersaturation (>0.4): The crystal MUST keep growing. Like a supersaturated solution — there's too much dissolved material for the crystal to stay this small.
Low supersaturation (<0.2): Equilibrium. The crystal is complete.
We decompose supersaturation into two components:
Structural energy: Probability mass on function words, connectors, punctuation → grammar demands completion
Semantic energy: Probability mass on content words → the topic has more to say

props.title: Stage 3: Structure Discovery

children.children: Here's where it gets genuinely novel. The supersaturation fingerprint reveals what kind of text structure the branch belongs to — after the fact.

After 'Paris is the capital of France,' the masks light up with 'London,' 'Berlin,' 'Rome' → Discovery: 'I'm in a LIST of European capitals.' A parent node [ENUMERATION] is retroactively created, and it constrains subsequent branches to follow the same parallel structure.

After 'Water evaporates from the ocean surface,' the masks light up with 'because,' 'which leads to,' 'as a result' → Discovery: 'I'm in a CAUSAL CHAIN.' Each subsequent branch must follow causally from its predecessor.
The tree structure isn't predicted top-down before generation — it's discovered bottom-up during generation. This is the key innovation.

Think of a snowflake's first arm. Its hexagonal structure tells you the crystal system — you now know what all the other arms will look like. Similarly, the first branch's supersaturation fingerprint tells you the discourse structure, and that structure constrains every subsequent branch.

props.title: 5. What We Actually Observed

children.children: We ran experiments on EC2 with a Tesla T4 GPU, starting with a small 0.6B parameter diffusion model (Qwen3-0.6B-diffusion-mdlm) and scaling up to LLaDA-8B-Instruct.

props.title: Finding 1: Content nucleates before scaffolding (at small scale)

children.children: With the 0.6B model, we watched token-by-token crystallization. For the prompt 'What is the capital of France?':
Step 9: 'Paris' resolves (confidence 0.94) while the rest is still all masks.
Step 10: 'The capital of' fills in around it.
Step 11: 'France is' completes the sentence.
The high-information content word crystallized first, then grammatical scaffolding grew around it — like a crystal nucleating at a seed point.

props.title: Finding 2: At larger scale, structure comes first

children.children: With the 8B model, the pattern reversed. Function words crystallized at average step 14.5, content words at step 35.4. Root verbs resolved at steps 0-2. Main clauses before subordinate clauses (step 18.3 vs 24.1).
The larger model builds the grammatical skeleton first, then fills in content — like how a developing embryo forms the body plan before growing specific organs.

props.title: Finding 3: Depth-first is cleaner AND faster

children.children: Head-to-head comparison on 10 prompts with LLaDA-8B:

Breadth-First    Depth-First
Clean outputs:         8/10            9/10
Quality issues:           2               1
Avg time:             12.9s            4.7s (2.7× faster)

Breadth-first produces garbled multi-sentence text ('atmosphere atmosphere, so it more scatter'). Depth-first produces clean sentences because each branch has full attention on all prior completed context.

props.title: Finding 4: Supersaturation is overwhelmingly semantic

children.children: When we decomposed the remaining energy at branch boundaries, it was almost entirely semantic (topic-level), not structural (grammar-level). The model finishes its grammar cleanly but topics have unresolved depth.
This means the continuation signal is genuinely about 'there's more to say on this topic' — not 'the grammar is incomplete.' The crystal knows when the idea is finished.

props.title: 6. The v5c Experiment: Pushing to Multi-Paragraph

children.children: Our latest experiment (v5c) pushed the system to longer, multi-sentence responses on 6 complex prompts — immune system, Apollo 11, French Revolution, evolution, government types, and climate change.
The results tell a clear story about what works and what doesn't yet:

props.title: Where dendritic diffusion shines

children.children: Baseline flat (breadth-first) generation was a repetition catastrophe on most prompts. The immune system prompt produced 'immunity' repeated 465 times. The evolution prompt got stuck in an 'and, and, and,' loop 210 times.

Dendritic diffusion, by contrast, kept repetitions to 2-4× of short phrases — the kind of mild redundancy you'd see in a first draft, not a repetition death spiral. For Apollo 11, the DD output produced a coherent three-branch narrative: the historical context, the crew's experience, and the challenges they faced.

props.title: Where it still fails

children.children: On two prompts (immune system and government types), dendritic diffusion produced nothing at all — zero characters. The crystal growth mechanism was too conservative and never nucleated a branch.

This is actually a meaningful failure mode: the system would rather produce nothing than produce garbage. The GROW/STOP energy thresholds need loosening so branches aren't pruned before they can produce content.

The crystal growth diagnostics show the supersaturation mechanism working in real time. For the evolution prompt: Branch 0 starts at sat=0.480 (high energy → GROW), each branch crystallizes with visible semantic content and structural energy decomposition. When energy drops, the system signals STOP.

props.title: 7. Why This Is a Diffusion Model Made from an AR Model's Cousin

children.children: Here's what makes this particularly intriguing. We're not training a new architecture from scratch. We're taking an existing masked diffusion language model — which was pre-trained with a transformer, the same architecture as GPT — and wrapping it in a generation procedure that fundamentally changes how it produces text.
The model itself doesn't know it's doing 'dendritic diffusion.' It just sees masked tokens and predicts what should go there. All the magic is in the orchestration:
WHICH tokens we mask (branch-by-branch instead of all-at-once)
WHAT context we provide (all previously crystallized branches)
WHEN we stop (supersaturation measurement, not a fixed length)
HOW we structure the output (emergent tree discovery from supersaturation fingerprints)

This is a profound point: the same weights, the same parameters, the same model — but a different generation procedure produces qualitatively different output. The intelligence isn't just in the weights. It's in how you USE the weights.

Standard masked diffusion models (LLaDA, Dream) already exist as alternatives to AR models. They share the transformer architecture but were trained with a different objective: predict masked tokens instead of next tokens. We take these models and add the dendritic generation procedure — turning a flat, breadth-first denoiser into a depth-first crystal grower.

props.title: 8. The Deeper Connection: How This Relates to How Transformers Actually Work

children.children: Our mechanistic interpretability work revealed something surprising about how transformers handle uncertainty internally. It turns out dendritic diffusion is doing explicitly what the transformer tries to do implicitly — and failing at.

props.title: The exception handler as failed crystallization

children.children: In GPT-2, Layer 11 has a set of 7 'consensus neurons' that act as a quorum. When they all agree, the MLP does almost nothing — the token passes through linearly. When consensus breaks, an exception handler (neuron N2123) fires and the full nonlinear processing kicks in.

This is the transformer's crude attempt at what supersaturation measurement does smoothly. Consensus = low supersaturation (crystal at equilibrium, pass through). Exception = high supersaturation (needs processing). But the transformer only gets one shot — binary routing, no iteration.
Dendritic diffusion gives the model multiple shots to get it right within each branch, and the supersaturation signal is continuous, not binary.

props.title: Knowledge neurons are actually routing neurons

children.children: A recent experiment showed that so-called 'knowledge neurons' in the MLP don't actually store facts. They're routing neurons — 36.5× enriched for routing patterns, and knocking them out INCREASES fact probability (by removing interference). Knowledge arrives through attention, not MLP storage.
This maps directly to dendritic branching: routing neurons don't hold the content, they gate what flows through each branch. The content emerges from the diffusion process itself.

props.title: 9. What's Next

children.children: The v5c results show the mechanism works but needs refinement. The immediate next steps:

Loosen GROW/STOP thresholds: The system is too conservative — it produces nothing rather than risk low-quality output. The empty outputs on 2/6 prompts are threshold calibration issues, not architectural failures.

Emergent structure classifier: Build the supersaturation fingerprint → discourse type mapping (LIST, CAUSAL_CHAIN, CLAIM_EVIDENCE, COMPARISON) and test whether parent constraints actually improve sibling coherence.
Garden-path immunity test: Feed the system classic garden-path sentences and verify that depth-first resolution prevents the misparse that AR models commit to.
SAE feature monitoring: Track which sparse autoencoder features activate at each denoising step — do they reveal the semantic → structural crystallization order at the feature level?

The bigger vision: text generation as crystal growth. Not a metaphor — a literal description of the algorithm. The text nucleates, grows along preferred semantic directions, and the completed crystal's geometry reveals the discourse structure that was latent in the prompt all along.

props.title: 10. The Landscape

children.children: Where does dendritic diffusion sit relative to other approaches?

Approach          Structure       Resolution Order       Branch Interaction
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
AR (GPT)          Flat sequence   Fixed left-to-right    None (unidirectional)
MDLM/LLaDA        Flat sequence   Confidence-first (BF)  None (independent)
Block Diffusion   Fixed chunks    L→R blocks, diff in    Sequential blocks
Dendritic (ours)  Semantic tree   Depth-first salient    Resolved → constrains

Every prior approach either has no structure (AR, standard diffusion) or imposes structure top-down before generation (plan-then-execute systems). Dendritic diffusion is the first to discover structure bottom-up during generation.

props.title: Summary

children.children: We took a masked diffusion language model — a transformer trained to predict masked tokens — and changed how it generates text. Instead of denoising all positions at once (breadth-first), we crystallize one branch at a time (depth-first), measure the remaining energy (supersaturation), and discover the discourse structure as it emerges.

The result: cleaner text, 2.7× faster generation, and a natural mechanism for multi-paragraph coherence that breadth-first diffusion can't achieve. The failure mode is under-generation (too conservative), not the repetition catastrophes that plague both AR and breadth-first diffusion on long outputs.

The name is literal: dendritic means tree-like. The generation process grows a tree — not by predicting the tree top-down, but by discovering it bottom-up during crystallization. Just like a real crystal.

questions.question: What is the main problem with breadth-first diffusion for multi-sentence text?
options: Each position denoises independently without seeing resolved neighbors, causing garbled output
It requires too much GPU memory
questions.explanation: In breadth-first diffusion, all positions denoise simultaneously. Position 5 doesn't know that position 12 just resolved to 'Paris,' leading to repetitions and broken grammar in multi-sentence output.

questions.question: What does 'supersaturation' measure in dendritic diffusion?
options: How much GPU memory the model is using
The number of masked tokens remaining
The residual confidence/energy at branch boundaries — whether there's more to say
The perplexity of the generated text
questions.explanation: Supersaturation measures the model's confidence on hypothetical continuation masks. High supersaturation means the 'solution' has too much dissolved material for the crystal to stay this small — the text must keep growing. It decomposes into structural (grammar needs completion) and semantic (topic has more to say) components.

questions.question: How is discourse structure (lists, causal chains, comparisons) determined in dendritic diffusion?
options: It's predicted top-down from the prompt before generation begins
It's hard-coded based on the type of question asked
It's discovered bottom-up from supersaturation fingerprints after each branch crystallizes
It's copied from training examples with similar prompts
questions.explanation: This is the key innovation. After a branch crystallizes, the supersaturation fingerprint reveals what kind of structure it belongs to — a list, causal chain, comparison, etc. The parent node is created retroactively and constrains subsequent branches. Structure is discovered, not planned.

questions.question: In the v5c experiments, what was the dendritic diffusion system's primary failure mode?
options: Producing nothing at all — the GROW/STOP thresholds were too conservative
Generating text in the wrong language
questions.explanation: On 2 of 6 prompts, dendritic diffusion produced zero characters. The crystal growth mechanism was too conservative and never nucleated. This is the opposite failure mode from baseline (which repeated tokens hundreds of times) — under-generation rather than over-generation.
